s: World's largest brick warehouse: 27 million bricks, 14 floors, 36 acres.
w: Built in 1901 for Liverpool's tobacco trade, 27 million bricks and 8,000 tons of steel went into its 14 storeys, covering 36 acres — at the time the largest building in the world by area. Floors hang on cast-iron columns above the dock basin. Derelict for decades, now flats and a hotel. The scale still floors you.
_facts: Grade II listed; world's largest brick warehouse with 1.6 million sq ft net floor area
Built 1901, 14 storeys, 125 ft high, spanning 36 acres
Construction used 27 million bricks, 30,000 panes of glass and 8,000 tons of steel
At time of construction claimed to be the world's largest building by area
Adjacent to Stanley Dock and the Leeds and Liverpool Canal

s: Paddy's Wigwam: the concrete funnel Liverpool never expected to love.
w: Lutyens designed a dome to dwarf St Paul's — only the crypt got built. In 1962 Gibberd's stained-glass lantern tower rose instead, and the roof promptly leaked. The cathedral sued for GBP1.3 million. Locals christened it Paddy's Wigwam and the Mersey Funnel. The Lutyens crypt is open to visit. Grade I listed.
_facts: Completed 1967, designed by Sir Frederick Gibberd; consecrated 14 May 1967
Nicknamed 'Paddy's Wigwam' and 'The Mersey Funnel'
Lutyens' original cathedral plan abandoned after only the crypt was built in 1958 — too costly
Cathedral sued Frederick Gibberd for GBP1.3 million over roof leaks and defective mosaic tiles
Grade I listed; upgraded June 2025

s: Britain's largest cathedral; its architect was 22, with no buildings to his name, and a Catholic.
w: Giles Gilbert Scott was 22 when chosen in 1903 — with no completed buildings, his only construction a pipe-rack made by his sister, and a Roman Catholic to boot, designing an Anglican cathedral. Work began 1904 and finished 1978. At 188 metres it is the longest cathedral in the world. Scott also designed the red telephone box.
_facts: Largest cathedral in Britain and 8th largest church in the world; longest in the world at 188.67 m
Designed by Giles Gilbert Scott, who was 22 years old with no completed buildings when chosen in 1903
Scott was Roman Catholic — designing an Anglican cathedral; his only prior construction was a pipe-rack made by his sister
Construction ran 1904-1978, taking 74 years
Scott also designed the British red telephone box and Battersea Power Station

s: A whole city block with a hidden arcade: the council demanded it be built through the middle.
w: Built 1924-32 for the Blue Funnel shipping line, occupying an entire block. Liverpool Corporation demanded a public arcade on the route of a demolished street — so the building was designed around a passage. A bomb hit it in 1941; one of its original architects oversaw the restoration. The arcade and Beaux-Arts lobby survive. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Built 1924-32 for Blue Funnel Line at a cost of GBP1.25 million
Occupies an entire city block; Italian Renaissance meets American Beaux-Arts style
Liverpool Corporation required an internal shopping arcade on the route of demolished Chorley Street
Badly damaged by bombing in 1941; restored under supervision of original architect Herbert Rowse
Contains the entrance to James Street underground station; Grade II* listed

s: The 19th-century alleyway pub where Lennon courted Cynthia.
w: Down Rice Street, a narrow offshoot of Hope Street — 'cracke' being a Liverpudlian word for an alleyway. John Lennon and Stuart Sutcliffe were regulars while at the College of Art; Lennon courted Cynthia Powell here in the late 1950s. Low ceilings, real ale, no fuss. The pre-Beatle local, barely changed.
_facts: 19th-century pub on Rice Street, off Hope Street; name from Liverpudlian word for an alleyway
John Lennon and Stuart Sutcliffe were regulars while at Liverpool College of Art in the late 1950s
Lennon courted his first wife Cynthia Powell here

s: The Liver Birds are chained up top — if they ever fly, the city floods.
w: Completed 1911 in reinforced concrete — one of the first buildings in the world built that way. Twin towers reach 90 metres, each topped by a Liver Bird. Legend has the male facing the sea so sailors return; the female facing the city so the pubs stay open. The birds are chained. Should they fly, Liverpool floods. Grade I listed.
_facts: Completed 1911, one of the world's first reinforced concrete buildings
Twin clock towers at 90 metres, topped by mythical Liver Birds adopted as symbols of Liverpool
Legend: male Liver Bird faces the sea (for sailors), female faces the city (for pubs)
Said the birds are chained; if they ever fly away, Liverpool will flood
One of Liverpool's Three Graces at the Pier Head; Grade I listed

s: Where trans-Atlantic passengers checked in before sailing for New York.
w: Built 1914-17 as Cunard Line HQ, it handled the passage of emigrants and first-class passengers departing Liverpool for New York. The Italian Renaissance facade conceals ornate sculptures on all sides. Rival White Star's building stands diagonally across the Strand. Cunard HQ until the 1960s; now the British Music Experience is inside. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Built 1914-17 as Cunard Line headquarters; Italian Renaissance and Greek Revival style
Cunard's passenger facilities for trans-Atlantic Liverpool departures were based here until 1960s
Diagonally across from Albion House (White Star Line HQ), Cunard's great rival
Grade II* listed; part of Liverpool's former UNESCO World Heritage designation
Now contains the British Music Experience among other tenants

s: When Titanic sank, officials read the dead from the balcony — too afraid to face the crowd.
w: Built 1896-98 as White Star Line HQ, red brick and Portland stone by Richard Norman Shaw. When news of the Titanic reached these offices in 1912, staff barricaded themselves inside and read the names of the deceased from the balcony to the crowd below. A South America mosaic covers the James Street entrance floor. Now a Titanic-themed hotel.
_facts: Built 1896-98 for White Star Line; designed by Richard Norman Shaw and J. Francis Doyle
When Titanic disaster news arrived 1912, officials read names of the dead from the balcony to waiting crowds
South America mosaic set into floor near the James Street entrance
Grade II* listed; Titanic registered in Liverpool though it never visited the port
Converted to 30 James Street Titanic-themed hotel in 2014

s: Concert hall and crown court under one roof, with dungeons underneath.
w: Opened 1854, Harvey Lonsdale Elmes's Neoclassical masterpiece fuses a concert hall with law courts. Prisoners were held in cells beneath, reached by tunnel from the courts. The Great Hall floor — 30,000 Minton encaustic tiles — is kept covered and revealed only occasionally. One of the finest neo-Grecian buildings in the world. Grade I listed.
_facts: Opened 1854; designed by Harvey Lonsdale Elmes, completed by C.R. Cockerell; Grade I listed
Combines concert halls and law courts in one Neoclassical structure
Criminal cells beneath connected to courts by tunnel
Great Hall contains 30,000 Minton encaustic tiles, normally covered for protection
Considered one of the finest neo-Grecian buildings in the world

s: Largest Chinese arch outside China, marking Europe's oldest Chinese community.
w: Liverpool's Chinatown dates to the 1860s — the oldest Chinese community in Europe, built largely on the Blue Funnel Line's trade routes. The paifang on Nelson Street, erected in 2000 by craftsmen from Shanghai, is the largest multiple-span arch of its type outside China. Four dragons guard its five spans. Hard to miss; even harder to walk past without stopping.
_facts: Liverpool's Chinatown is the oldest Chinese community in Europe, dating to the 1860s
The paifang on Nelson Street is the largest multiple-span arch of its kind outside China
Erected 2000 by craftsmen from Shanghai with four dragons across five spans
Community grew largely from the Blue Funnel Line's trade routes between Liverpool and the Far East

s: Where Lennon's first known photo was taken — for a tabloid 'Beatnik Horror' exposé.
w: John Lennon moved into No. 3 with Stuart Sutcliffe in 1960, both at the College of Art. A Sunday People reporter visited to write 'This is the Beatnik Horror' and inadvertently produced the first known published photograph of John Lennon. The Georgian terrace, built 1832-37 by John Foster Junior, overlooks St James's Cemetery and the cathedral. Grade II* throughout.
_facts: Georgian terrace by John Foster Junior, built 1832-37; Grade II* listed throughout
John Lennon lived at No. 3 with Stuart Sutcliffe in 1960 while at Liverpool College of Art
Sunday People 'This is the Beatnik Horror' (July 1960) inadvertently produced the first known published photograph of Lennon
Cynthia Lennon also attended the junior art school housed at nos. 11-12
Overlooks St James's Mount, Gardens and Liverpool Cathedral

s: Live turtles, a Titanic lounge, Churchill and Sinatra: faded grandeur on an epic scale.
w: When it opened in 1914 it was regarded as the most luxurious hotel outside London. The 1876 predecessor kept live turtles in heated basement tanks for turtle soup. The Sefton Suite is said to be an exact replica of the Titanic's First Class Smoking Lounge. Guests: Roosevelt, Churchill, Sinatra, Bob Dylan. Currently under Britannia Hotels. Still magnificent.
_facts: Opened 1914; designed by Frank Atkinson; regarded as 'most luxurious hotel outside London' at opening
1876 predecessor kept live turtles in heated basement tanks for turtle soup supplied to banquets nationwide
The Sefton Suite is said to be an exact replica of the RMS Titanic's First Class Smoking Lounge
Guests have included Roosevelt, Churchill, Frank Sinatra, Bob Dylan, Judy Garland, Laurel and Hardy
Grade II listed; built on site of Ranelagh Gardens, Liverpool's first public open space

s: The Sex Pistols played Liverpool once. It was here, on opening night, 1976.
w: Opened 1 October 1976 with The Sex Pistols (their only Liverpool gig) and The Runaways. Directly opposite the Cavern Club on Mathew Street, Eric's hosted The Clash, Joy Division, Buzzcocks, Ramones and Talking Heads. Echo & the Bunnymen, The Teardrop Explodes and OMD all found their feet here. Named to sound the opposite of 'Tiffany's'. Still open.
_facts: Opened 1 October 1976; first night featured The Runaways and The Sex Pistols in their only Liverpool gig
Located on Mathew Street opposite the original Cavern Club site
Hosted Joy Division, The Clash, Buzzcocks, Talking Heads, and the Ramones
Launched Echo & the Bunnymen, Julian Cope, The Teardrop Explodes, OMD, and Dead or Alive
Named by Ken Testi as deliberate antidote to disco club names like 'Tiffany's'

s: Where Willy Russell, Jonathan Pryce and Pete Postlethwaite all came up.
w: Founded in Hope Hall in 1964 with a political edge and a bohemian reputation. Willy Russell's 'Blood Brothers' and 'Educating Rita' began here; Jonathan Pryce, Pete Postlethwaite, Antony Sher and Julie Walters all passed through. Rebuilt 2011-14, the new building won the RIBA Stirling Prize. The original ethos, defiantly, held.
_facts: Founded 1964 in Hope Hall; quickly built reputation for ground-breaking politically engaged work
Willy Russell developed 'Blood Brothers' and 'Educating Rita' here
Alumni include Jonathan Pryce, Pete Postlethwaite, Antony Sher and Julie Walters
Completely rebuilt 2011-14; new building won RIBA Stirling Prize in 2014
Managed together with the Liverpool Playhouse since 1999

s: Fire took the 1849 original; Beecham opened the Art Deco replacement in 1939.
w: The first Philharmonic Hall opened 1849 and burned down in 1933. Herbert Rowse's Art Deco replacement opened in 1939 with Sir Thomas Beecham conducting. An acoustic shell drops from the ceiling like a stage set. The building faces the Philharmonic pub diagonally across Hope Street — one of the great corner relationships in Britain. Grade II* listed.
_facts: Current hall opened 1939; designed by Herbert Rowse; predecessor destroyed by fire in 1933
Opened by conductor Sir Thomas Beecham; home of the Royal Liverpool Philharmonic Society (founded 1840)
Grade II* listed; distinctive acoustic shell suspended from the ceiling
Diagonally faces the Grade I listed Philharmonic Dining Rooms pub on Hope Street

s: Music hall from 1866, repertory from 1911: Rex Harrison got his start here.
w: Opened as a music hall in 1866, it became a repertory theatre in 1911 — among the first in Britain. Rex Harrison and Michael Redgrave appeared in the 1930s. A modern extension grafted on in 1968 was not universally loved. Joined in management with the Everyman since 1999. Williamson Square outside was once a cattle market.
_facts: Originated as a music hall in 1866; became a repertory theatre in 1911, among Britain's earliest
Rex Harrison and Michael Redgrave appeared here in the 1930s
Modern extension added 1968; managed together with the Everyman Theatre since 1999
Located in Williamson Square, historically a cattle market

s: Liverpool's 'Harley Street of the North' — and an Egyptian pyramid lurks at the top.
w: Over 60 Grade II listed Georgian buildings line this street where doctors have practised since the 19th century — Liverpool's answer to Harley Street. Gladstone was born at No. 62 in 1809. The churchyard at the top contains an Egyptian pyramid tomb: the mausoleum of a wealthy cotton broker said locally to be full of treasure. Unconfirmed.
_facts: Over 60 Grade II listed buildings; known as the 'Harley Street of the North'
William Gladstone (four-times Prime Minister) born at No. 62 in 1809
St Andrew's Church churchyard contains an Egyptian pyramid tomb of a cotton broker
Part of the Rodney Street conservation area with Hope Street and Gambier Terrace

s: The Tate's first outpost outside London, inside an 1846 cast-iron dock warehouse.
w: When Tate Liverpool opened in 1988 in a converted Albert Dock warehouse it was the first Tate gallery outside London. James Stirling designed the internal conversion; the shell dates to 1846, cast iron and brick. The dock basin reflects the building on good days. Free entry for the permanent collection. The Albert Dock complex is Grade I listed.
_facts: Opened 1988 as the first Tate gallery outside London; an initiative of the Merseyside Development Corporation
Housed in an Albert Dock warehouse dating to 1846; Albert Dock is Grade I listed
Internal conversion designed by James Stirling
Displays Tate Collection of British art from 1500 and international modern art

s: The only park in England flanked by two Premier League grounds: Anfield and Goodison.
w: Edward Kemp's 110-acre Victorian park opened in 1870, wedged between Anfield and Goodison Park. On derby day, Liverpool and Everton fans walk through the same paths to their separate gates. The 1899 Isla Gladstone Conservatory — a Grade II listed iron and glass structure by Mackenzie & Moncur of Edinburgh — sits in the eastern corner. Neutral ground.
_facts: 110-acre park designed by Edward Kemp, opened 14 May 1870
Located between Anfield (Liverpool FC) and Goodison Park (Everton FC) — only park in England flanked by two Premier League grounds
Contains the 1899 Isla Gladstone Conservatory, Grade II listed iron and glass structure by Mackenzie & Moncur
Includes a former horse-riding track restyled as a cycle track c.1907

s: Paxton's park template of 1842: the design chain that led to Central Park, New York.
w: Opened 1842, designed by Joseph Paxton and James Pennethorne — Paxton's first urban park commission, five years before his Birkenhead Park, which directly influenced Frederick Law Olmsted when he created Central Park. The serpentine lake and carriage drive set a template for Victorian city parkmaking. Richard Yates gifted it to Liverpool in 1849. Grade II* Historic Park.
_facts: Designed by Joseph Paxton and James Pennethorne; opened 1842
Paxton's first urban park commission; his later Birkenhead Park (1847) directly influenced Olmsted's Central Park, New York
Serpentine lake and carriage drive established a style widely emulated in Victorian urban parks
Gifted to the city by developer Richard Vaughan Yates in 1849
Grade II* Historic Park; named for Edward, Prince of Wales

s: Liverpool's Confederate embassy: the square where merchants funded the American South.
w: Built 1819-30, this Georgian square became the operational centre for Confederate sympathisers during the American Civil War. No. 19 housed Charles Prioleau of Fraser, Trenholm & Co, Confederate agents who funded blockade-running, known as the unofficial embassy of the Confederacy. Jefferson Davis visited Liverpool three times after the war, sometimes staying here. Now a University of Liverpool garden.
_facts: Georgian square designed from 1819 by John Foster Sr, completed c.1830
No. 19 was home to Charles K. Prioleau, Confederate agent who funded blockade-running; regarded as 'unofficial embassy' of the Confederacy
Former Confederate president Jefferson Davis visited Liverpool three times post-war, sometimes staying in Abercromby Square
Named after General Sir Ralph Abercromby, who died at the Battle of Alexandria in 1801
Now central to the University of Liverpool campus

s: UK's largest two-tier auditorium: the Beatles played here before their first TV appearance.
w: Opened 1925, the Empire has the largest two-tier auditorium in the UK with 2,348 seats. The Beatles played here on 28 June 1963, the night before their first major national TV appearance. Chuck Berry, the Rolling Stones and virtually every 20th-century touring act came through this stage. The original ceiling dome survives.
_facts: Opened 1925; second theatre on the Lime Street site
Largest two-tier auditorium in the UK, seating 2,348
The Beatles played 28 June 1963 here, the night before their first major national TV appearance
Acts including Chuck Berry, Rolling Stones and nearly every major 20th-century touring act performed here
